commands.notes: Limited to the authorized requester’s own orders.

commands.description: Cancels an activation by activation ID.
commands.notes: Also available from inline action buttons.

commands.notes: Also shows inline selection buttons for top countries.
Also available from inline action buttons.

commands.description: Lists the public commands and practical examples.
commands.notes: Owner-only /status is shown only to owners.

commands.description: Creates one or more HeroSMS activation orders concurrently.
examples: User: /order service=wa country=philippines qty=3 → Bot: per-order success/failure summary
commands.example: User: /order service=wa country=philippines qty=3 → Bot: per-order success/failure summary
commands.notes: Supports operator, maxPrice, fixed, and qty.

commands.description: Shows pricing and availability for a service in a country.
examples: User: /prices service=wa country=vietnam → Bot: prices payload
commands.example: User: /prices service=wa country=vietnam → Bot: prices payload

commands.notes: Also available from inline action buttons.

examples: User: /services philippines → Bot: service codes and names
commands.example: User: /services philippines → Bot: service codes and names

examples: User: /start → Bot: HeroSMS OTP bot for authorized users...
commands.example: User: /start → Bot: HeroSMS OTP bot for authorized users...
commands.notes: Unauthorized users receive a denial message.

examples: Owner: /status → Bot: active counts, defaults, polling, webhook URL
commands.example: Owner: /status → Bot: active counts, defaults, polling, webhook URL

aiMessages.content:
Built a new Telegram-only HeroSMS bot project with grammY, MongoDB persistence, HTTP webhook routes in the same process, owner-gated authorization, concurrent order creation, polling recovery, inline activation actions, safe logging, and synced docs/env samples.

Files touched (35): .env.sample, DOCS.md, README.md, package.json, src/bot.js, src/commands/active.js…
Validation: ✅ project passes syntax/basic checks after this edit.

planMarkdown:
Bot: Telegram Bot Architecture for Concurrent HeroSMS Number Ordering and OTP Tracking

Summary:
This plan describes a production-ready Telegram bot built with Node.js ESM and grammY that lets authorized users request temporary numbers from HeroSMS for WhatsApp-focused activations, with emphasis on Philippines and Vietnam, and strong support for concurrent order handling.

The bot is designed as a single Node.js service that integrates Telegram, HeroSMS, MongoDB, and an internal job orchestration layer. Its core responsibilities are: accepting order requests, validating country and pricing preferences, creating and tracking multiple simultaneous activations, polling or reconciling provider status, delivering OTP updates back to Telegram, and exposing operational controls for cancellation, completion, and monitoring.

The design assumes HeroSMS behaves as an external provider with inconsistent response formats across endpoints, including plain text and JSON. Therefore, the system includes a normalization layer that converts provider responses into a stable internal domain model. Concurrency is handled inside the single process through a controlled async task scheduler, activation-specific locks, and MongoDB-backed state so that multiple user orders can progress independently without collisions.

The resulting bot is intended for reliable operational use: clear user-facing order lifecycle states, admin visibility, bounded retries, provider error categorization, and safeguards around duplicate updates, thread limits, rate pressure, and process restarts.

Product goals:
The bot’s primary goal is to make HeroSMS number ordering usable from Telegram with minimal friction for repeated operational use. The user should be able to request a WhatsApp-compatible activation in a preferred country, receive the purchased number quickly, monitor its status, and get OTP updates in the same chat. Since the user explicitly wants a focus on Philippines and Vietnam, the product should optimize the UX around these two countries first, while keeping the country/service model generic enough to support expansion later.

The second goal is concurrency. The bot must support multiple simultaneous orders across users and multiple simultaneous orders per user, subject to configurable policy. Concurrency here means more than allowing parallel commands: the system must safely track many active activations at once, prevent duplicate processing of the same activation, avoid race conditions when polling or changing status, and remain responsive even when HeroSMS returns delays or transient errors.

The third goal is operational clarity. Users need a simple lifecycle: requested, ordered, waiting for code, code received, completed, canceled, failed, or expired. Admins need visibility into current active orders, provider balance, common failure causes, and whether the system is hitting provider-side limits such as CHANNELSLIMIT or bans. The product should therefore expose both user commands and admin commands for health and oversight.

A final goal is resilience against provider inconsistency. HeroSMS mixes plain text responses, structured JSON, legacy-compatible methods, and newer methods. The bot should prefer the richer V2 endpoints where practical, but normalize all provider outputs into internal states so the Telegram interface remains consistent regardless of which endpoint is used or how HeroSMS responds.

High-level architecture:
The system should be organized into six logical layers inside one Node.js process.

1) Telegram interface layer. This is the grammY bot responsible for command parsing, authorization, conversational prompts if needed, callback query handling, and sending status updates. It should remain thin and delegate all business logic to application services.

2) Application service layer. This layer owns order creation, activation lifecycle transitions, user quotas, country defaults, pricing checks, and outbound notifications. It translates user intent into domain actions such as create order, reserve number, mark ready, request resend, cancel activation, complete activation, and fetch OTP history.

3) HeroSMS integration layer. This layer is a provider adapter for all HeroSMS endpoints used by the bot. It should encapsulate URL construction, API key usage, timeout handling, response parsing, normalization of text and JSON responses, and provider-specific error mapping. The rest of the system should never depend directly on raw HeroSMS response formats.

4) Concurrency and orchestration layer. Since the bot runs as a single process, concurrency should be managed through an internal scheduler using async tasks with bounded parallelism. There should be separate concepts for: a global provider request concurrency cap, per-user active order caps, and per-activation locks. Each activation should only have one in-flight lifecycle mutation at a time. Polling work should be partitioned by activation state and next-check time to avoid hammering the provider.

5) Persistence layer. MongoDB should be used as the source of truth for users, orders, activations, events, configuration, and audit logs. In-memory maps are still useful for short-lived runtime concerns such as locks, cooldown timers, and deduplication caches, but durable state must live in MongoDB so the process can recover after restarts.

6) Observability and admin layer. This layer records structured events for each provider request, activation transition, user command, and notification sent. It also powers admin commands such as balance check, active orders, failed orders, and provider error summaries.

A practical runtime model is event-driven with scheduled polling. When a user creates an order, the bot persists an order record, attempts provider purchase, then spawns lifecycle tracking for that activation. A scheduler continuously scans MongoDB for activations due for status checks. Due activations are claimed by the single process through in-memory lock keys plus optimistic state update conditions in MongoDB. This prevents duplicate progression even if multiple parts of the bot attempt action on the same activation.

Webhook support can be treated as optional. The HeroSMS documentation mentions inbound SMS webhooks configured in the provider account, but since the stack requirement is a single Node.js service, the bot may expose a small HTTP endpoint within the same process if the deployment environment allows inbound HTTP. If inbound HTTP is not guaranteed, the production baseline should rely on polling with adaptive intervals. The architecture should support both, using the same normalization and event ingestion path so either polling or webhook creates the same activation update event.

Data model:
The data model should center around Order, Activation, User, and Event records.

1) User collection. Stores Telegram identity and policy data. Suggested fields include telegramUserId, username, displayName, role, isActive, defaultCountry, allowedCountries, maxConcurrentOrders, createdAt, updatedAt, and optional notes. If the bot is private-use, this also serves as the authorization registry.

2) Order collection. Represents a user request from Telegram. Suggested fields include orderId, telegramUserId, requestedService, requestedCountry, requestedOperator, maxPrice, fixedPrice, priority, requestSource, status, statusReason, createdAt, updatedAt, and linked activationIds. Order status should be bot-centric, such as pendingpurchase, purchased, waitingcode, codereceived, completed, canceled, expired, failed. This is the object shown to the user.

3) Activation collection. Represents the provider-side activation. Suggested fields include activationId from HeroSMS, orderId, providerName, providerServiceCode, providerCountryCode, phoneNumber, operator, activationCost, currency, providerRawStatus, normalizedStatus, code, smsText, codeHistory, activationTime, activationEndTime, canRequestAnotherSms, pollAfter, lastPolledAt, lastProviderResponseAt, completedAt, canceledAt, errorClass, errorCode, errorDetails, retryCount, and version. This object is the lifecycle engine’s main unit.

4) Event collection. Stores append-only domain events for observability and debugging. Suggested event types include commandreceived, ordercreated, providerpurchaseattempted, providerpurchasesucceeded, providerpurchasefailed, activationpolled, activationstatuschanged, otpreceived, notificationsent, activationcompleted, activationcanceled, and schedulererror. Each event should link to relevant user, order, and activation IDs and include a compact payload.

5) Config collection. Stores runtime configuration and defaults that admins may adjust without redeploying. Suggested fields include defaultServiceCode for WhatsApp, preferredCountries ordered list, per-country max price defaults, polling intervals by state, global request concurrency cap, per-user concurrency cap, and admin chat IDs.

6) Runtime in-memory maps. These are not durable and should only hold ephemeral process state. Suggested maps include activationLockMap keyed by activationId, userActionCooldownMap keyed by telegramUserId and command, providerRequestSemaphore state, and messageDedupMap keyed by activationId and OTP content hash.

Country handling should use internal aliases for Philippines and Vietnam mapped to HeroSMS numeric country codes discovered from getCountries. Since provider country IDs should not be hardcoded blindly, the system should maintain a cached country reference collection refreshed on demand or periodically. The same applies to service codes. For WhatsApp, the actual service code must be confirmed from getServicesList and stored as configuration rather than assumed.

The activation status model should normalize provider responses into a small internal state machine. Suggested normalized states are: purchased, markedready, waitingcode, waitingretry, waitingresend, codereceived, canceled, finished, expired, providererror, manualreview. This prevents UI complexity caused by HeroSMS mixed raw responses.

Key flows:
1) User onboarding and authorization. A user starts the bot, the bot checks whether the Telegram user is allowed, and returns the available commands and country shortcuts. For private use, unauthorized users receive a generic denial message. Authorized users can set defaults, such as Philippines or Vietnam as their preferred country.

2) Create order flow. The user issues a command to order a number for WhatsApp, optionally specifying country, operator, quantity intent, and max price. The bot validates policy, confirms the service code mapped to WhatsApp, and creates an Order in pendingpurchase state. The application layer then submits a purchase request to HeroSMS, ideally using getNumberV2 for richer metadata. If the provider succeeds, the bot persists the Activation, links it to the Order, and immediately replies with the phone number, price, activation ID, and current waiting status.

3) Concurrent order handling flow. Multiple orders can be created close together by one or more users. The scheduler should enforce a global outbound request concurrency limit to avoid overloading the provider or tripping account restrictions. It should also enforce per-user active order caps to prevent accidental abuse. Each successful activation is tracked independently with its own next poll time. Per-activation locks ensure that polling, cancel, complete, and resend actions cannot overlap incorrectly.

4) Activation tracking flow. After purchase, the bot may optionally call setStatus with status 1 to indicate readiness where required by the provider workflow. The scheduler then polls getStatus or getStatusV2 using adaptive intervals. Early in the lifecycle, polling can be more frequent; after repeated waits, it should slow down slightly. When STATUSOK or equivalent structured data arrives, the code and full message are stored, a deduplicated notification is sent to Telegram, and the activation is moved to codereceived.

5) OTP delivery flow. When an OTP arrives, the bot sends a concise message containing the phone number, code, raw message text if available, country, service, and activation ID. If multiple OTPs arrive for the same activation, the system appends them to codeHistory and sends only genuinely new data. Duplicate webhook deliveries or repeated poll results must not create repeated user notifications.

6) Completion flow. Once the OTP is received and the user confirms they are done, or if auto-finish policy is enabled, the bot marks the activation complete via finishActivation or legacy setStatus with status 6 depending on configured strategy. The final state is written to MongoDB, the order is marked completed, and polling stops.

7) Cancel flow. A user may cancel an activation manually. The system first checks whether cancellation is allowed based on local state and provider constraints. It then calls cancelActivation or setStatus status 8, updates records, and stops polling. If the provider rejects cancellation due to free cancellation expiry or OTP already received, the user gets a precise message and the activation remains in an appropriate state.

8) Balance and market intelligence flow. Admins can request current provider balance, top prices, and available operators for Philippines and Vietnam. The integration layer queries getBalance, getPrices, getOperators, and optionally getTopCountriesByService. Results are normalized into concise Telegram output useful for operational decisions.

9) Recovery flow after restart. On process startup, the scheduler scans MongoDB for activations in active states such as purchased, waitingcode, waitingretry, waitingresend, and codereceived-not-finished. It reconstructs runtime tracking, recalculates next poll times, and resumes processing. In-memory locks are rebuilt lazily as work is claimed.

10) Error handling flow. Provider errors are classified into retryable, terminal, and policy-related categories. Examples: NONUMBERS and transient server issues are retryable within bounded limits; BADKEY is terminal and should trigger admin alerting; CHANNELSLIMIT or BANNED should pause new purchases and surface operational warnings; NOBALANCE should halt new purchases and instruct admins to replenish the provider account. All such errors are recorded as events and reflected in order status reasons.

Tech stack:
The implementation should strictly use the following stack.

1) Runtime and language. Node.js using ESM modules.

2) Telegram framework. grammY, using TELEGRAMBOTTOKEN from environment variables.

3) Persistence. MongoDB as the primary durable datastore. Simple in-memory maps may be used only for ephemeral locks, deduplication, and rate-control helpers inside the single process.

4) External integration. HTTPS client calls from the Node.js service to HeroSMS API endpoints. A dedicated provider adapter should normalize both plain text and JSON responses.

5) Scheduling and concurrency. In-process async scheduler with bounded concurrency. No external queues, brokers, or additional infrastructure. All job orchestration remains within the single Node.js service.

6) Optional inbound webhook handling. If deployment allows inbound HTTP on the same Node.js server, a lightweight HTTP endpoint may be included in the same process to receive provider webhooks. This is optional and should share the same activation update pipeline as polling.

7) File handling. Not required for the core OTP bot. If export or report artifacts are ever needed, use simple filesystem storage or object storage in generic terms only.

8) Deployment. A single Node.js server in the cloud, with environment variables for bot token, HeroSMS API key, MongoDB connection string, admin IDs, and runtime limits.

This design intentionally does not introduce any other database, cache, broker, or queueing system.

Non-functional requirements:
Reliability should come from idempotency, bounded retries, and durable state transitions. Every provider response should be safe to process more than once. Notifications should be deduplicated. Activation state changes should be persisted atomically enough to avoid sending contradictory updates after restarts.

Performance should focus on handling many active activations concurrently within one process. The scheduler must avoid a naive tight polling loop. Instead, it should use pollAfter timestamps, adaptive intervals based on activation state, and a global request semaphore. This keeps Telegram responsive while the bot monitors many orders in parallel.

Security should include strict environment variable management, least-privilege admin access, and no exposure of provider credentials in Telegram messages or logs. If webhook handling is enabled, requests should be validated as far as the provider allows, and the endpoint should be isolated to only the minimal payload handling needed.

Maintainability should be driven by clean boundaries: Telegram handlers, application services, provider adapter, scheduler, repositories, and event logging. Raw HeroSMS formats should never leak past the integration layer. This is especially important because the provider mixes legacy text responses with structured JSON and may evolve over time.

Observability should include structured logs, event records in MongoDB, and admin-facing status commands. At a minimum, the operator should be able to answer: how many active orders exist, how many are waiting for code, what the latest provider balance is, which country is failing most often, and whether the account is rate-limited or banned.

Operational safety should include configurable limits: maximum active orders per user, maximum global active orders, maximum provider requests per second, maximum retries per failed purchase, and timeout thresholds for stale activations. For Philippines and Vietnam, country-specific defaults can improve UX and reduce user error.

Finally, user experience should remain concise and deterministic. Each order should have a stable bot order ID, a clear current state, and obvious next actions such as refresh, cancel, resend request, or complete. When failures occur, the bot should translate provider jargon into actionable messages while still storing the raw provider error for admin review.

Bot commands:
• /start — Registers the chat session, checks authorization, and shows the bot’s core capabilities with country shortcuts for Philippines and Vietnam. (usage: /start) [general]
• /help — Shows concise usage help, supported order flow, activation lifecycle, and available admin actions if the user has permission. (usage: /help) [general]
• /order — Creates a new WhatsApp activation order through HeroSMS with optional country, operator, and max price settings. Intended as the primary command for production use. (usage: /order country=ph service=whatsapp maxPrice=0.50 operator=any) [ordering]
• /orderph — Shortcut to create a WhatsApp-focused order with Philippines defaults. (usage: /orderph maxPrice=0.50 operator=any) [ordering]
• /ordervn — Shortcut to create a WhatsApp-focused order with Vietnam defaults. (usage: /ordervn maxPrice=0.50 operator=any) [ordering]
• /orders — Lists the user’s recent and active orders with bot order ID, country, phone number, current state, and latest OTP summary. (usage: /orders) [ordering]
• /active — Shows currently active activations being tracked for the user, useful when many concurrent orders exist. (usage: /active) [tracking]
• /status — Fetches the latest normalized status for a specific bot order or activation and shows whether the system is waiting, received an OTP, canceled, or completed. (usage: /status <orderId|activationId>) [tracking]
• /otp — Displays the latest OTP and message text for a given order or activation, including any previously received messages if available. (usage: /otp <orderId|activationId>) [tracking]
• /refresh — Triggers an immediate status refresh for a specific activation outside the normal scheduler cadence, subject to cooldown rules. (usage: /refresh <orderId|activationId>) [tracking]
• /resend — Requests another SMS where supported by the provider lifecycle and updates the activation to a waiting-for-resend state. (usage: /resend <orderId|activationId>) [actions]
• /cancel — Attempts to cancel an activation purchase and stop tracking, returning the provider’s reason if cancellation is no longer allowed. (usage: /cancel <orderId|activationId>) [actions]
• /finish — Marks an activation as completed after OTP use and stops further polling or notifications for that activation. (usage: /finish <orderId|activationId>) [actions]
• /setdefault — Sets personal defaults such as preferred country or max price to speed up repeated ordering. (usage: /setdefault country=ph maxPrice=0.50) [preferences]
• /prices — Shows current availability and pricing for the configured service in Philippines and Vietnam, optionally filtered by country. (usage: /prices [country=ph|vn]) [market]
• /operators — Lists available telecom operators for the selected country so the user can narrow ordering preferences. (usage: /operators country=ph) [market]
• /balance — Shows the current HeroSMS account balance. Typically restricted to admins or trusted operators. (usage: /balance) [admin]
• /adminactive — Displays all active activations across users, including counts by state and country, for operational monitoring. (usage: /adminactive) [admin]
• /adminerrors — Shows recent provider failures grouped by error code, such as NONUMBERS, NOBALANCE, CHANNELSLIMIT, or BANNED. (usage: /adminerrors) [admin]
• /adminconfig — Views or updates runtime configuration such as default countries, concurrency limits, and polling intervals. (usage: /adminconfig show) [admin]

1) Runtime and language. Node.js using ESM modules.

2) Telegram framework. grammY, using TELEGRAMBOTTOKEN from environment variables.

3) Persistence. MongoDB as the primary durable datastore. Simple in-memory maps may be used only for ephemeral locks, deduplication, and rate-control helpers inside the single process.

4) External integration. HTTPS client calls from the Node.js service to HeroSMS API endpoints. A dedicated provider adapter should normalize both plain text and JSON responses.

5) Scheduling and concurrency. In-process async scheduler with bounded concurrency. No external queues, brokers, or additional infrastructure. All job orchestration remains within the single Node.js service.

6) Optional inbound webhook handling. If deployment allows inbound HTTP on the same Node.js server, a lightweight HTTP endpoint may be included in the same process to receive provider webhooks. This is optional and should share the same activation update pipeline as polling.

7) File handling. Not required for the core OTP bot. If export or report artifacts are ever needed, use simple filesystem storage or object storage in generic terms only.

8) Deployment. A single Node.js server in the cloud, with environment variables for bot token, HeroSMS API key, MongoDB connection string, admin IDs, and runtime limits.

This design intentionally does not introduce any other database, cache, broker, or queueing system.
planSections.nonFunctional:
Reliability should come from idempotency, bounded retries, and durable state transitions. Every provider response should be safe to process more than once. Notifications should be deduplicated. Activation state changes should be persisted atomically enough to avoid sending contradictory updates after restarts.

Performance should focus on handling many active activations concurrently within one process. The scheduler must avoid a naive tight polling loop. Instead, it should use pollAfter timestamps, adaptive intervals based on activation state, and a global request semaphore. This keeps Telegram responsive while the bot monitors many orders in parallel.

Security should include strict environment variable management, least-privilege admin access, and no exposure of provider credentials in Telegram messages or logs. If webhook handling is enabled, requests should be validated as far as the provider allows, and the endpoint should be isolated to only the minimal payload handling needed.

Maintainability should be driven by clean boundaries: Telegram handlers, application services, provider adapter, scheduler, repositories, and event logging. Raw HeroSMS formats should never leak past the integration layer. This is especially important because the provider mixes legacy text responses with structured JSON and may evolve over time.

Observability should include structured logs, event records in MongoDB, and admin-facing status commands. At a minimum, the operator should be able to answer: how many active orders exist, how many are waiting for code, what the latest provider balance is, which country is failing most often, and whether the account is rate-limited or banned.

Operational safety should include configurable limits: maximum active orders per user, maximum global active orders, maximum provider requests per second, maximum retries per failed purchase, and timeout thresholds for stale activations. For Philippines and Vietnam, country-specific defaults can improve UX and reduce user error.

Finally, user experience should remain concise and deterministic. Each order should have a stable bot order ID, a clear current state, and obvious next actions such as refresh, cancel, resend request, or complete. When failures occur, the bot should translate provider jargon into actionable messages while still storing the raw provider error for admin review.

envVarDocs.title: Telegram Bot Token
envVarDocs.description: Telegram bot token used by grammY for long polling.
envVarDocs.usage: Required at boot. If missing, the service exits with guidance.
envVarDocs.howToGet: Create a bot with BotFather on Telegram.

envVarDocs.title: MongoDB Connection URI
envVarDocs.description: MongoDB connection string used for persistence of orders, events, users, and webhook dedupe.
envVarDocs.usage: Required at boot for this bot because persistence is mandatory.
envVarDocs.howToGet: Use a MongoDB Atlas or self-hosted MongoDB connection string.

envVarDocs.title: HeroSMS API Key
envVarDocs.description: API key used to authenticate HeroSMS requests through the api_key query parameter.
envVarDocs.usage: Required for all HeroSMS balance, catalog, and activation actions.
envVarDocs.howToGet: From your HeroSMS account API settings.

envVarDocs.title: HeroSMS Base URL
envVarDocs.description: Base endpoint for HeroSMS API calls.
envVarDocs.howToGet: Usually keep the default unless HeroSMS provides a custom endpoint.

envVarDocs.title: Bot Owner IDs
envVarDocs.description: Comma-separated Telegram user IDs with owner access and automatic authorization.
envVarDocs.usage: Used for owner-only commands and initial authorization.
envVarDocs.howToGet: Get your Telegram numeric user ID from Telegram tools or another bot.

envVarDocs.title: HTTP Port
envVarDocs.description: Port used by the built-in HTTP server for health and webhook routes.
envVarDocs.usage: Falls back safely to 3000 when missing.
envVarDocs.howToGet: Usually provided automatically by your hosting platform.

envVarDocs.title: Public Base URL
envVarDocs.description: Public URL of the deployed service used to display webhook setup instructions.
envVarDocs.usage: Optional. The bot still runs when it is missing.

envVarDocs.title: HeroSMS Webhook Secret
envVarDocs.description: Shared secret used to protect the incoming HeroSMS webhook route.
envVarDocs.usage: If missing, the webhook route stays disabled without crashing.
envVarDocs.howToGet: Choose and configure the same secret in your HeroSMS webhook sender setup.

envVarDocs.title: Default Country Priority
envVarDocs.description: Ordered country names used to prioritize country listings and defaults.
envVarDocs.howToGet: Set a comma-separated list of country names.

envVarDocs.title: Default Service Code
envVarDocs.description: Default service code used by /order when service is omitted.
envVarDocs.howToGet: Get the service code from HeroSMS services list.

envVarDocs.title: Default Max Price
envVarDocs.description: Optional default max price for new orders.
envVarDocs.howToGet: Set a numeric ceiling matching your pricing strategy.

envVarDocs.title: Polling Interval
envVarDocs.description: Polling interval in milliseconds for active activation tracking.
envVarDocs.usage: Falls back safely to 15000 and is clamped to a conservative minimum.
envVarDocs.howToGet: Set based on your desired balance between speed and provider load.

envVarDocs.title: Polling Batch Limit
envVarDocs.description: Maximum number of active orders processed per polling cycle.
envVarDocs.howToGet: Set based on your provider limits and order volume.

commandDocs.description: Shows welcome text, access scope, and the main commands.
examples: User: /start → Bot: HeroSMS OTP bot for authorized users...
commandDocs.notes: Unauthorized users receive a denial message.

commandDocs.description: Lists the public commands and practical examples.
commandDocs.notes: Owner-only /status is shown only to owners.

commandDocs.description: Lists available countries with Philippines and Vietnam prioritized.
commandDocs.notes: Also shows inline selection buttons for top countries.

examples: User: /services philippines → Bot: service codes and names
commandDocs.notes: Country can be a name or ID.

commandDocs.description: Shows pricing and availability for a service in a country.
examples: User: /prices service=wa country=vietnam → Bot: prices payload

commandDocs.description: Creates one or more HeroSMS activation orders concurrently.
examples: User: /order service=wa country=philippines qty=3 → Bot: per-order success/failure summary
commandDocs.notes: Supports operator, maxPrice, fixed, and qty.

commandDocs.description: Shows the user’s active activation orders.
commandDocs.notes: Limited to the authorized requester’s own orders.

commandDocs.description: Cancels an activation by activation ID.
commandDocs.notes: Also available from inline action buttons.
Also available from inline action buttons.
Also available from inline action buttons.

commandDocs.description: Owner-only command to show defaults and basic runtime status.
examples: Owner: /status → Bot: active counts, defaults, polling, webhook URL
commandDocs.notes: Only BOT_OWNER_IDS can use this command.
